                                                                      Agricultural Systems 195 (2022) 103285


                                                                     Contents lists available at ScienceDirect


                                                                       Agricultural Systems
                                                            journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/agsy


Research Paper

Crop-livestock integration provides opportunities to mitigate
environmental trade-offs in transitioning smallholder agricultural systems
of the Greater Mekong Subregion
B.K. Paul a, *, C.A. Epper a, b, c, D.J. Tschopp b, C.T.M. Long d, V. Tungani a, D. Burra e, h, L. Hok f,
P. Phengsavanh g, S. Douxchamps h
a
  International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT), Tropical Forages Program, Nairobi, Kenya
b
  ETH Zurich, Group of Plant Nutrition, Eschikon, Lindau, Switzerland
c
  Agroscope, Research Division Agroecology and Environment, Zurich, Switzerland
d
  Western Highlands Agriculture & Forestry Science Institute (WASI), Buon Ma Thuot, Viet Nam
e
  Yara International ASA, Bengaluru, India
f
  Royal University of Agriculture (RUA), Phnom Penh, Cambodia
g
  National Agriculture and Forestry Research Institute (NAFRI), Vientiane, Laos
h
  International Center for Tropical Agriculture (CIAT), Tropical Forages Program, Hanoi, Viet Nam




H I G H L I G H T S                                        G R A P H I C A L A B S T R A C T

• The Greater Mekong Subregion is un­
  dergoing rapid agricultural trans­
  formation, accompanied by negative
  environmental impacts
• This study explores the potential of
  crop-livestock integration to mitigate
  such agro-environmental trade-offs
• Nitrogen balances and greenhouse gas
  emissions were partly determined by
  transition stage, but not agricultural
  diversity
• Crop-livestock integration resulted in
  larger ‘solution spaces’ to mitigate
  trade-offs than business as usual
• Investment in research and extension is
  needed to develop and scale context-
  specific    crop-livestock    integration
  practices




A R T I C L E I N F O                                      A B S T R A C T

Editor: Dr Val Snow                                        CONTEXT: The Greater Mekong Subregion has been undergoing rapid agricultural transformation over the last
                                                           decades, as traditional diverse subsistence-oriented agriculture is evolving towards intensified commercial
Keywords:                                                  production systems. Negative environmental impacts often include deforestation, nutrient pollution, and
Agricultural diversity                                     greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions.
Greenhouse gas emissions
Multi-objective optimization



    * Corresponding author.
      E-mail address: birthe.paul@giz.de (B.K. Paul).

https://doi.org/10.1016/j.agsy.2021.103285
Received 30 October 2020; Received in revised form 24 September 2021; Accepted 25 September 2021
Available online 23 October 2021
0308-521X/© 2021 The Author(s).             Published by Elsevier Ltd.        This is an open access                   article   under   the   CC   BY-NC-ND   license
(http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).
B.K. Paul et al.                                                                                                               Agricultural Systems 195 (2022) 103285

Nutrient balances
                                                OBJECTIVE: This study aims to explore the potential of crop-livestock integration to mitigate trade-offs between
Sustainability
Farming systems modeling                        economic and environmental impacts of smallholder farming systems at different stages of agricultural transition
                                                and degrees of agricultural diversity across the Greater Mekong Subregion.
                                                METHODS: We chose a ‘middle ground’ between detailed modeling of few, representative farming systems and
                                                modeling of large household populations. 24 low and high diversity farms were selected in Laos (Xieng Khouang
                                                province), Cambodia (Ratanakiri province) and Vietnam (Central Highlands) from a survey dataset of 1300
                                                households. These farming systems were simulated with the whole-farm bio-economic and multi-objective
                                                optimization model FarmDESIGN, calculating operating profit, GHG emissions and nitrogen (N) balance. Two
                                                optimizations (‘business as usual’ vs. ‘crop-livestock integration’) were performed, generating ‘solution spaces’ or
                                                alternative configurations aiming to maximize profitability, keep farm N balanced and minimize GHG emissions.
                                                RESULTS AND CONCLUSIONS: Agricultural systems across the sites differed in their production orientation and
                                                management practices, representing various stages of agricultural transition. Nitrogen balances varied between
                                                sites, being negative in Ratanakiri (average − 20.5 kg N ha− 1 y− 1) and Xieng Khouang (− 36.5 kg N ha− 1 y− 1) and
                                                positive in the Central Highlands (73 kg N ha− 1 y− 1). Negative balances point to unsustainable mining of nu­
                                                trients due to sale of cash crops without sufficient inputs, and positive balances to the risk of environmental
                                                contamination. Total GHG emissions ranged from 0.52–8.12 t CO2e ha− 1 and were not significantly impacted by
                                                stage of agricultural transformation or agricultural diversity. GHG sources in Ratanakiri and Xieng Khouang were
                                                determined by crop residue burning while in Central Highlands fertilizer and livestock were main emitters. High
                                                diversity farms obtained higher operating profits (10,379 USD y− 1) than low diversity farms (4584 USD y− 1).
                                                Crop-livestock integration, a combination of measures including introduction of improved forages grasses,
                                                manure recycling and residue feeding, and reduction of residue burning, resulted in larger ‘solution spaces’, thus
                                                providing farmers with more options to mitigate agro-environmental trade-offs.
                                                SIGNIFICANCE: These findings underline the potential of crop-livestock integration to support sustainable
                                                intensification pathways in the Greater Mekong region. Public and private investment in further research and
                                                extension is needed to develop and scale context-specific crop-livestock integration practices.




1. Introduction                                                                   fuels. Critics have been arguing that despite the economic development
                                                                                  that the Green Revolution spurred in South-East Asia, its high reliance
    The Greater Mekong Subregion, comprising the countries along the              on external inputs such as fossil fuels and agrochemicals led to decreased
Mekong River Basin in Southeast Asia, has been undergoing stark and               environmental health, and more variable farm productivity and incomes
rapid agricultural and socio-economic transformation over the last de­            (Ramankutty et al., 2018).
cades. Traditional diverse and subsistence-oriented agriculture is                    Sustainable intensification has been proposed by science and policy

from 14.7 million in 2013 to 15.3 in 2019 (NIS, 2019). The country                significant departure from business as usual (Peterson and Snapp, 2015).
reached lower middle-income status in 2015 and its economy was one of             System redesign is considered to be essential to deliver optimum eco­
the fastest growing in the world during 1999–2018, with an average                nomic and ecological outcomes, going beyond increased efficiency and
growth rate of 8% per annum (Asian Development Bank, 2019). In the                substitution (Pretty et al., 2018). Sustainable agricultural intensification

holds have decreased from 94% to 80% (FAO, 2019), either by tran­                 profitability (Peterson and Snapp, 2015; Tilman et al., 2011). The
sitioning out of agriculture or intensifying towards market-oriented              integration of crops and livestock is often seen as one ingredient for
agriculture (Bouahom et al., 2004). The economic and population                   sustainable intensification as it offers multiple management and sus­
growth, combined with infrastructure development, improved market                 tainability benefits including provision of nutrients for crop production
access and government policies, has resulted in different profound and            through manure, provision of draft power and crop residues and forage

tural intensification and commercialization have often been accompa­              and the increased use of animal manure for crop fertilization have been

2. Materials and methods
                                                                                   (Brachiaria ruziziensis, Pennisetum purpureum). Although cattle fattening

2.1. Study sites
                                                                                   consumption or as asset in case of sudden cash need. In Ratanakiri,
                                                                                   smallholder farmers have started to cultivate more cash crops, due to
    Three sites were selected to represent different stages of agricultural
                                                                                   improved road infrastructure with Vietnam, increasing attraction of
transition from subsistence orientation to commercial production,
                                                                                   external investors, and conversion of forest and pasture into rubber,
following previous characterization in Ritzema et al. (2019) and Burra
                                                                                   cashew and cassava plantations. Similar to Laos, poultry and pigs were
et al. (2021). Ratanakiri province in Cambodia, Xieng Khouang province

in Laos, and the Central Highlands in Vietnam are characterized by

specific farming practices, climatic and topographic conditions

(Table 1).

    Smallholder farming was dominant in all three sites, although pro­
                                                                                   tence to market orientation in the last three decades, particularly after
duction orientation and management practices differed. In Xieng
                                                                                   the prohibition of the shifting cultivation practice in 1981 (Salemink,
Khouang province, smallholder crop-livestock farms were mainly
                                                                                   2003). Rice and vegetables were mostly produced for household con­
subsistence-oriented, based on rice (Oryza sativa L.) and vegetables
                                                                                   sumption. The local government has supported meat production and
(different varieties in home gardens), with few cash crops (tea, maize,
                                                                                   most farmers have intensified from extensive grazing to specialized,
banana and chili). Slash-and-burn practices remained common, while
                                                                                   crossbred cattle species fattening practices and the production of
mineral or organic fertilizers were not widely used. Poultry and pigs
                                                                                   improved cut-and-carry forage.
were left free during the day and additionally fed with maize grains
bought at the local market. Cattle were mainly left grazing on the own
pasture land and additionally fed with local or improved forages

2.2. Household survey and selection                                                 parameterization of degradation, nutrient losses and organic matter
                                                                                    (OM) breakdown (Groot et al., 2012). The inputs required for the model
    Two broad approaches to whole farm modeling and trade-off anal­                 can be grouped into: (i) biophysical environment (e.g. soils, climate); (ii)
ysis can be distinguished: detailed modeling of few farming systems,                socio-economics (e.g. input costs, labour price); (iii) crops and crop
types or classes that are considered representative, or quick calculations          products yield, composition and use; (iv) livestock and livestock prod­
across a population of households. In this study, we decided for ‘middle            ucts yield, composition and use; (v) manure types and degradation, and
ground’, aiming to combine the strengths of both approaches through                 mineral fertilizer use; (vi) household members and labour availability.
not omitting variability within types while at the same time being able to              In this study, farm performance was evaluated in terms of farm ni­
gain insights into detailed dynamics. We therefore relied on a large                trogen (N) balance, greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions and operating
survey dataset of more than 1300 households from where we derived a                 profit using the same system boundaries as in Epper et al. (2020). The
farming systems classification based on agricultural diversity. Then, we            indicators for the 24 farms were calculated as follows:
proceeded to in-depth understanding of the farming system types by                                   (              )
                                                                                    Nitrogen balance kg N ha− 1 y− 1 (input–output)
modeling a larger-than-usual set of 24 households to have replicates per
farming system. The procedure and methods are described in the

following.

conducted in February and March 2017, allowed identifying agricultural

    Agricultural diversity is defined in this study as the sum of crop and
livestock diversity, with one count per one crop or one livestock species.          = Gross margin crops + gross margin livestock − fixed costs − variable costs
Home gardens are also counted as one, although we acknowledge that                                                                                                          (2)
those can harbour various different crops that are important for nutri­
tional diversity. The diversity score was calculated for each household,            where gross margin crops and gross margin livestock: the products that
and households were then categorized into two distinct farm types: Low              the farmers produce on their farm and that could potentially be sold;
diversity (LD) and high diversity (HD). The threshold between low and               fixed costs: land rent, equipment and building costs – on our study
high diversity was site-specific, based on frequencies of each diversity            assumed to be zero in this study as farmers mainly cultivate on their own
score across the entire RHoMIS dataset (see Supplementary Material                  land and use minimal equipment; variable costs: refer to expenses for
SM1), and validated by local experts. Low diversity was defined as <6               fertilizer, crop protecion, green manure, feed and hired casual labour.
for Xieng Khouang, and <5 for Ratanakiri and Central Highlands, while               Exchange rates of 4085 Cambodian Riel (KHR), 8692 Lao kip (LAK),
high diversity was set to be >7 for Xieng Khouang and >6 for both                   23,208 Vietnamese Dong (VDN) to the US Dollar were applied (ex­
Ratanakiri and Central Highlands. Farms falling into the middle range               change rates from October 2019).
values, or “buffer zone”, were excluded. RHoMIS survey villages with                    The GHG emission module of FarmDESIGN is described in detail in
important representation of each type were selected for visits in each              Paul et al. (2019), relying on a combination of IPCC Tier 1 and 2
site, during which farms were randomly selected, subject to accessibility           methods. In brief, it includes the following GHG sources: (i) methane
and availability of the farmers, for a total of four farms per type and site,       (CH4) from livestock enteric fermentation, (ii) CH4 and direct and in­
or 24 farms. Fig. 1 indicates the locations (Fig. 1a) and the diversity             direct nitrous oxide (N2O) from manure storage and application, (iii)
scores of the modeling farms (red lines) against the entire household               N2O from mineral fertilizer application; (iv) direct and indirect N2O
dataset (Fig. 1b).                                                                  from soils through N input from crop residue retention, N fixation and
    The same data collection process described in Epper et al. (2020) was           atmospheric deposition, (v) CO, CO2, N2O, NOx and CH4 from burning
used to interview the 24 case study farms. In brief, all farmers were               of organic material. Input data on livestock numbers, manure produc­

(Rufino et al., 2009) to collect quantitative data on assets, farm pro­             multiplied with IPCC Tier 1 emission factors. N manure excretion rate

study).                                                                             Calculated N2O and CH4 emissions were converted into CO2 equivalents

2.3. Agro-environmental indicators                                                  21 for CH4 and 310 for N2O. Off-farm GHG emissions, for example for
                                                                                    fertilizer or feed production, are not taken into account.
    The 24 case study farms were simulated using the whole-farm bio-                    Farm characteristics and agro-environmental indicators were
economic optimization model FarmDESIGN (Groot et al., 2012), which                  compared using a combination of farm type (LD referring to Low Di­
calculates the impacts of various farm configurations on a large set of             versity and HD referring to High Diversity) and study region (Ratanakiri,
agro-environmental and socio-economic performance indicators. Ap­                   Xieng Khouang and Central Highlands). Since the sample size within
plications globally have suggested the model is robust enough to                    each farm type and region was low (i.e. n = 4), and the residuals of the
accommodate various agro-environments and farming systems, and its                  response variables, i.e. agro-environmental and economic characteris­
functionality has been illustrated in South-East Asia in various recent             tics (Nitrogen Balance, GHG emissions/ha and Operating profit) did not
studies (Birnholz et al., 2017; Epper et al., 2020; Ditzler et al., 2019;           exhibit normal distribution, we employed non-parametric tests to test
Estrada-Carmona et al., 2020; Timler et al., 2020). FarmDESIGN has                  for significant differences in agro-environmental and economic vari­
been evaluated in terms of design-, output- and end-user validity.                  ables, between regions, between farm types and between farm types
However, uncertainty lies in the quality of input data, as well as                  within each region. Therefore, we first performed an analysis that

2.4. Multi-objective optimization                                                            timizations, land area could not be increased more than 5% than the
                                                                                             currently managed area, and the livestock feed at maintenance must be
   FarmDESIGN contains a multi-objective Pareto-based optimization                           guaranteed. The crop-livestock intensification scenario represents an
algorithm that can evaluate and minimize trade-offs between several                          alternative development pathway that has been encouraged by policies
production objectives. The model generates clouds of alternative farm                        and programs throughout the region. In Cambodia, one of the strategic
configurations, based on available resources and provided with a limited                     focuses of the government is to push the investment in research devel­
room and decision variables to reallocate these (Groot et al., 2012).                        opment of high value-added crops, livestock and aquaculture to improve
   Two optimization runs were applied to the 24 farms (Table 2). The                         agricultural productivity, quality and diversification (Royal Govern­
aim of both multi-objective optimizations in this study was set to                           ment of Cambodia, 2018). The Agricultural Development Strategic Plan
maximize the operating profit, minimize GHG emissions, maximize                              2019–2023 aims to modernize farming practices and to build more
                                                                                             infrastructure to become more competitive and resilient to climate
                                                                                             change, thus enhancing productivity, diversifying potential crops and
Table 2
                                                                                             commercialization (MAFF, 2019). In the Central Highlands of Vietnam,
Objectives, decision variables and main constraints for two optimization runs for
24 farms in FarmDESIGN: business as usual vs. crop-livestock integration.
                                                                                             local government advocates for development of the beef value chains to
                                                                                             meet the high domestic demand and create economic opportunities for
  Optimizations    Business as usual                 Crop-livestock integration
                                                                                             smallholder farmers (Stür et al., 2013). Dak Lak and Dak Nong’s Pro­
  Objectives       • Minimize GHG emissions                                                  vincial People Committee explicitly encourages crop diversification in
                   • Maximize operating profit
                                                                                             its provincial restructuring agricultural plans, and envisions the devel­
                   • Maximize organic matter balance
                   • Minimize (Central Highlands) or maximize (Ratanakiri, Xieng
                                                                                             opment of the livestock sector towards incentive systems by 2020
                     Khouang) the farm N balance                                             (MARD, 2009; Dak Lak PPC, 2016; Dak Nong PPC, 2018).

(Fig. 4a). Xieng Khouang farms on the contrary produced more crops for                biological fixation due to substantial soybean cultivation, balancing

livestock, particularly poultry, in all three provinces (Table 3). Usually            for the international market. Main N imports in this site were feed
farmers produced feed from their own farms, and only imported small                   (maize grain for poultry) and in one case manure from neighbors. In
quantities. Farms in Ratanakiri kept fewest livestock of which mostly                 Xieng Khouang, both LD and HD managed wild tea or tea plantations,

as asset and for ceremonies. Xieng Khouang farmers owned the largest                  the final N-balance as much as maize monoculture (Fig. 4a).

Khouang all kept at least one cow, and sometimes also a small herd                    Paddy rice production resulted in considerable GHG emissions from
which stays on the own pasture or grazes freely during the day. Farms in              most farms. GHG emissions from ruminants reflected the higher live­

relying on cattle fattening and improved breeds fed under cut-and-carry,              lands farms were the only ones applying mineral fertilizers at high rates,

sold to the market. Pigs were common in both Xieng Khouang and                        erably smaller (Fig. 4b).
Central highland farms and sold to the market as regular source of

                                                                                      3.3. Exploring solution spaces for crop-livestock integration vs. business as
                                                                                      usual
3.2. Economic and environmental performance of farming systems
                                                                                          The size of the ‘solution space’ or performance of alternative farm
    Mean nitrogen (N) balances across both HD and LD farms were                       configurations that the model generates provides indication for the
negative for Ratanakiri (average − 20.5 kg N ha− 1 y− 1) and Xieng                    number of options these farms have to navigate agro-environmental
Khouang farmers (− 36.5 kg N ha− 1 y− 1), and significantly differed from             trade-offs (Fig. 5). In both scenarios, business as usual (5b) and crop-
those of Central Highlands (73 kg N ha− 1 y− 1; Kruskal-Wallis chi-                   livestock integration (5d), a trade-off was apparent between profit­
squared = 10.573, df = 2, BH adjusted p-value <0.05 between Rata­                     ability and GHG emissions, though higher profitability could be ach­
nakiri and Central Highlands, BH adjusted p-value <0.05 between Xieng                 ieved with crop-livestock intensification under similar GHG emission
Khouang and Central Highlands). Highest N balance was found in the                    increases. The relationship between profitability and N balance was
Central Highlands (192 kg N ha− 1 y− 1), and lowest in Xieng Khouang                  more diverse (Fig. 5a and c). In general, HD farms had larger solution
(− 96 ha− 1 y− 1) (Fig. 3a). Total GHG emissions ranged from 0.52–8.12 t              spaces than LD farms. Slopes, incremental changes per unit profit, were
CO2e/ha. No significant differences in GHG emissions were observed,                   lower for HD farms than for LD farms, almost everywhere, showing that
either between regions, or between farm types, and even between farm                  the negative effects (increased GHG emissions or disbalanced N) were
types within each region (Fig. 3b). However, in the case of operational               less dramatic in HD farms for each increase in profit. Farms in Central
profits, HD farm types obtained higher (10,379 USD y− 1), and margin­                 Highlands had smaller solution spaces (often rather linear than scattered
ally significant operating profits in comparison to LD farm types (4584               spaces) than the farms in Ratanakiri and Xieng Khouang related to the
USD y− 1; Wilcoxon rank sum test, p-value <0.05; Fig. 3C). However, the               small available cropping areas in the Central Highlands which limits
same was not observed when comparing between LD and HD farm types                     their opportunities. In the ‘crop-livestock integration’ optimization
within each region. Neither did operating profits differ significantly                (Fig. 5c, d), the solution spaces and thus options to mitigate trade-offs
between the three regions (Fig. 3c).                                                  for all farms for both N balance and GHG emissions increased when

                                                                                  7
B.K. Paul et al.                                                                                                                        Agricultural Systems 195 (2022) 103285




Fig. 2. Cropping areas (a) and total livestock units (TLUs) (b) for 24 study farms in Ratanakiri (Cambodia), Xieng Khouang (Laos) and Central Highlands (Vietnam).
Crops and livestock in blue are mainly produced and kept for household consumption, while red colors indicate produce for the local or international market. Maize,
bananas and cassava are represented twice as they are often either used for home consumption or sold to the markets. The numbers above the stacked barplots
indicate total cropping area (ha) (a) and total TLUs (b). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of
this article.)




                                                                                      8
B.K. Paul et al.                                                                                                             Agricultural Systems 195 (2022) 103285




Fig. 3. Boxplots representing differences in (a) Nitrogen balance (b) GHG emissions and (c) operating profits between High Diversity (HD) and Low Diversity (LD)
farms located in Ratanakiri (Cambodia), Xieng Khouang (Laos) and Central Highlands (Vietnam).


4. Discussion                                                                      practice of cassava and maize residue burning. The prohibition of slash-
                                                                                   and-burn practices in the Central Highlands showed its effects, while the
4.1. Environmental performance under current agricultural practices                practice is still common in Ratanakiri and Xieng Khouang (Burra et al.,
                                                                                   2021). Total GHG emissions from the studied farms ranged from
    Agricultural systems in Ratanakiri, Xieng Khouang and Central                  0.52–8.12 t CO2e/ha, and were lower than for example in smallholder
Highlands differed in their production orientation and practices, rep­             crop-livestock systems in Central Kenya where average emissions ranged
resenting various stages of agricultural transition. In the Central High­          from 4.5 to 12.5 t CO2e/ha with highest emissions from farms with high
lands, farms have developed towards market-oriented systems, with                  livestock density and high input use (Ortiz-Gonzalo et al., 2017). See­
large input use and livestock produced for sale. Xieng Khouang in Laos             bauer (2014) found whole-farm emissions from Western Kenya ranging
and Ratanakiri in Cambodia display more convergent traits of transition,           from 5.9–10.1 t CO2e/ha, while Paul et al. (2018) calculated annual
having access to larger cropping areas and producing cash crops to a               GHG emissions of 0.4–1.5 t CO2e per household in Rwanda (Paul et al.,
varying degree, with dominance of home consumption in Xieng                        2018) and 2.9–16.2 t CO2e per household in Northern Tanzania (Paul
Khouang. Ratanakiri had hardly any livestock holdings except poultry               et al., 2019). Agricultural diversity did not consistently impact envi­
while livestock keeping in Xieng Khouang was mainly extensive and for              ronmental outcomes. This trend parallels with Ritzema et al. (2019) who
home consumption. Environmental impacts of agricultural production                 found dietary diversity not being determined by single consistent factors
per unit area could only be partly related to the stage of agricultural            across all sites, but influenced by different determinants in each site.
transition. Farmers in the Central Highlands had significantly higher N            Burra et al. (2021) also found various, context-specific drivers for
balances than in the other two regions. This is linked to processes of             complex land-use change in the Greater Mekong region. Impacts of
commercialization and small landholdings, and farmers have intensified             transition on dietary diversity, land use change and environmental
using large amounts of available and affordable mineral fertilizers.               impact appear site-specific and therefore require contextualized policies
Positive nutrient balances between 80 and 100 kg N ha− 1 have been                 and approaches.
found in other studies from Vietnam as well (Phong et al., 2010).
Although target values for N management and balances depend on
specific contexts, Quemada et al. (2020) suggest 80 kg N ha− 1 as an               4.2. Potential of crop-livestock integration to mitigate economic-
upper boundary beyond which environmental contamination is ex­                     environmental trade-offs
pected. N balances were mostly negative for Ratanakiri and Xieng
Khouang farmers, pointing to ongoing nutrient mining due to low input                  Crop-livestock integration referred to a package of options to the
use despite sale of cash crop products. In Ratanakiri, cropping land was           farms including more productive livestock herds, higher crop diversity
increased by relatively recent forest clearing so that nutrient replen­            including forage legumes and grasses, decrease of crop residue burning,
ishment has not yet become urgent. Legacy soil fertility, relatively large         more manure application to crops and residue feeding to livestock. This
cropping land sizes and wide-spread soybean cultivation still provide              was contrasted with a business as usual scenario that focused on
sufficient nutrients to sustain productivity in the meantime. Findings for         increasing cash crop production (Table 2). Crop-livestock integration
Xieng Khouang farmers are in line with Epper et al. (2020), who have               resulted in larger ‘solution spaces’, thus providing farmers with more
reported that the traditional soil fertility management technique, fal­            options to mitigate economic-environmental trade-offs. HD farms ten­
lows of 5–10 years, are not possible anymore but that the new, more                ded to have larger solution spaces than LD farms, and farms in Rata­
intensified cropping systems without nutrient replenishment is unsus­              nakiri had more options available while farmers in the Central
tainable. GHG emissions did not differ significantly between regions,              Highlands the least. These solution spaces can also be seen as wiggle
though the GHG sources varied. Main source in the Central Highlands                room, giving farms the space to maneuver and making them more
was fertilizer use, and in Ratanakiri and Xieng Khouang the common                 resilient. The more diverse a system, the more integrated it has the
                                                                                   potential to be, the greater the resilience through complementarity

Fig. 4. (a) N balance (kg N ha− 1 y− 1) across farm types and study sites in Ratanakiri (Cambodia), Xieng Khouang (Laos) and Central Highlands Vietnam. The
numbers above the stacked barplots represent the total N balance per farm. Fertilizer imp. refers to both animal manure and mineral fertilizers (a). GHG emissions (kg
CO2e ha− 1 y− 1) per year across farm types and study sites. The numbers above the stacked barplots represent the total GHG emission per farm and hectare (b).


mechanisms (Gil et al., 2017). Livestock, when well-managed and                       discussion and investment (Paul et al., 2020b). However, despite the
closely integrated with crop production, could potentially play an                    technical potential of such solutions that this and other modeling studies
important role to mitigate economic-environmental trade-offs by con­                  can illustrate, adoption at farm level is determined by additional,
verting residues into animal-source food and manure for fertilization                 practical factors. In the case of improved livestock feeding and forages,
while reducing the polluting practice of residue burning. A previous                  such factors include lack of awareness and knowledge, low support, and
study demonstrated that forage-based livestock fattening can increase                 lack of available and accessible inputs, labor and market linkages. More
operating profits by 35%, while maintaining soil organic matter and                   systemic issues around land tenure, challenging transitions of entire
decreasing GHG intensities (Birnholz et al., 2017). Timler et al. (2020)              production systems and differences in production objectives can also
demonstrated the potential for crop diversification options to satisfy                inhibit adoption of closer crop-livestock integration (Paul et al., 2020a).
household dietary needs and generate income gains in Vietnam, while
Estrada-Carmona et al. (2020) also point to trade-offs between achieving              4.3. Methodological reflections on whole farm modeling and optimization
both nutritional and economic objectives. This study underlined that
realizing the potential of livestock’s positive contribution to sustainable              In terms of methods, this study aimed to take the middle way be­
intensification will require a shift of perception towards a more nuanced             tween two basic contrasting approaches to whole-farm modeling of

                                                                                 10
B.K. Paul et al.                                                                                                                 Agricultural Systems 195 (2022) 103285




Fig. 5. ‘Solution spaces’ or performance of alternative farm configurations for 24 farms to achieve higher farm profitability, balanced N balance and lower GHG
emissions ‘business as usual’ (a and b) and ‘crop-livestock integration’ (c, d) across the three study sites Ratanakiri (Cambodia), Xieng Khouang (Laos) and Central
Highlands (Vietnam). For optimization settings and assumptions, see Table 2. The original farm configurations are given in black (OR), the possible solutions for low
diversity (LD) and high diversity (HD) farms are indicated in red and blue respectively.


smallholder systems: i) Detailed modeling of few farming system types                 approach, results are often presented in strata following the particular
or classes that are considered representative (typically <10), or ii) quick           research question. Strata could be based on research questions (Shikuku
calculations and modeling of a population of households (typically 30 -               et al., 2017), classes identified in the outcome variables such as food
>10,000). We relied on a large survey dataset of more than 1300                       availability or greenhouse gas emissions (e.g. Frelat et al., 2016; Paul
households from where we derived a farming systems classification                     et al., 2018; Ritzema et al., 2017), or types identified through multi­
based on agricultural diversity. Then, we proceeded to in-depth under­                variate statistics (Douxchamps et al., 2016; Falconnier et al., 2015;
standing of the farming systems types by modeling a larger-than-usual                 Lopez-Ridaura et al., 2018).
set of 24 households to have replicates per farming system. Table 5 il­                   The selection of our mixed methods aimed to combine the strengths
lustrates the two contrasting approaches with selected studies and their              of both approaches. Modeling of real and existing households allowed
geographic scope and number of farming systems/households modeled.                    for the modeling to be conducted in a participatory manner, fostering
Methods for selecting farms can be classified as ‘real’ versus ‘con­                  the dialogue with farmers between ‘actual’ and ‘desirable’ systems
structed’. By ‘real’, we refer to an actually existing farm that is consid­           (Prost et al., 2018). We could also verify and validate data used for
ered representative for the farming system type, which can be identified              model parameterization and calibration, and obtain a more in-depth
in various ways. It can be selected from a household dataset through                  understanding of dynamics and complexities. Having replicates for
multivariate statistics (e.g. Alary et al., 2016; Cortez-Arriola et al., 2014;        each farming system type enabled us to not omit variability within types,
Paul et al., 2019; Rigolot et al., 2017; Waithaka et al., 2006), or through           and we could perform statistical analysis on results that is often not
participatory Focus Group Discussions (Michalscheck et al., 2018). A                  possible when modeling of single households per type.
farm can also be purposively selected to test and illustrate functionalities              This study aimed to explore the potential of crop-livestock integra­
of a new model or modules (Ditzler et al., 2019; Parsons et al., 2011).               tion to mitigate trade-offs between economic and environmental per­
Generic or constructed farming systems, thus not corresponding to                     formance. Farming systems research is mainly seen here as a problem-
existing households, have been defined for large geographic areas using               solving process, where emphasis is placed on the computational explo­
various data sources including literature, policy documents, census data              ration of solution spaces (Martin et al., 2013). Drawing the boundaries
and expert knowledge (Mayberry et al., 2017, 2018) or from mean                       of these spaces is essential but we acknowledge that it is just the first step
values of household survey data for each particular type (Descheemaker                in a necessary further process to identify which practical solutions
et al., 2018). The second approach is modeling entire household pop­                  within these spaces are most appropriate and practically feasible. This
ulations. Relatively simple indicators, such as land productivity, food               step will have to be embedded in a participatory process, involving
availability and self-sufficiency, greenhouse gas emissions (IPCC Tier 1),            stakeholder with different backgrounds and skills, and working at
household income, or dietary diversity score can be calculated for all                different scales, to offer a more balanced view of issues and potential
households of a household survey. Although not necessary in this                      solutions. Far from linear, this is an iterative process of co-learning and

Selected studies illustrating two contrasting approaches to whole-farm modeling               The results of this study illustrate the potential of crop-livestock
of smallholder systems, including geographic scope and number of farms/                   integration to support much-needed sustainable intensification path­
households modeled.                                                                       ways in the Greater Mekong, where rapid commercialization is leading
